facts.text: Jasmine Robinson is a 44-year-old woman based in Miami.
facts.reasoning_path_text: First, the screenshot of a profile or account page provides direct OCR evidence for the owner's full name, showing 'Jasmine Robinson' alongside a birth date in 1968 and a Miami home address in Brickell. From the album window ending in 2012, a 1968 birth year supports an age of 44 during this period. Second, the work scene in Downtown Miami grounds that the named owner is the woman repeatedly seen in the album: her face is visible, and she is standing in a Miami workplace setting with Downtown Miami text and law-office context, linking the person in the pictures to the Miami-based life shown elsewhere. Third, the cycling meetup scene on Rickenbacker Causeway places the same recognisable woman in another Miami-specific routine setting on a different month, showing that Miami is not a one-off address artifact but the base of her recurring everyday life. Together these photos establish the identity Jasmine Robinson, support inference of age 44 from the readable birth date during the 2011-2012 album period, and show that she is based in Miami through both explicit address text and repeated grounded Miami locations. Her female presentation is visually evident across the owner-face scenes.

key_photo_roles.evidence_role: Direct OCR evidence gives her full name and a 1968 birth year, which supports age 44 during the 2012 portion of the album, and also provides a Miami home address.
key_photo_roles.planned_description: A close, slightly angled phone screenshot fills the frame, showing a secure profile or account page open on a bright screen resting on a dark kitchen counter in Jasmine's Brickell apartment. The interface is simple and utilitarian rather than social-media styled, with black text on a white background and a small circular profile thumbnail at the top. The readable account details include her full legal name, a date of birth line, and a mailing address line that clearly places her in Miami. The surrounding phone bezel, a faint reflection from overhead apartment lighting, and a blurred coffee mug near the edge make it feel like a real everyday capture rather than a synthetic graphic. The camera sees enough of the page to connect identity and city without making it an unrealistic all-in-one biography card. The birthday line provides the year needed to infer age during the 2011-2012 album period, while the Brickell address anchors her base within Miami. No face needs to be visible because the text itself is the evidence carrier.

full name line reading Jasmine Robinson
date of birth showing year 1968
Miami address line with Brickell

key_photo_roles.planned_description: Jasmine is photographed from a few steps away in the lobby or entrance area of a Downtown Miami office building, likely just after work. She is clearly recognisable as the album owner, wearing a fitted blazer over a sheath dress and holding a structured tote and a slim folder. The space has polished stone flooring, warm evening interior lighting, and a glass wall looking out toward city traffic. A directory panel or entrance sign behind her includes the words 'Downtown Miami,' making the location legible in the scene itself. She is turned slightly toward the camera with a natural, relaxed half-smile, not posing formally, and the frame includes enough surrounding architecture to read as a workplace district rather than a generic hallway. Reflections on the glass, a security desk off to one side, and other professionals moving in the background give the image lived texture. This photo visually ties the named identity from the text artifact to the same woman seen in Miami professional life and supports that her everyday base is in the city.

facts.text: She completed a trade certificate before building her legal career.
facts.reasoning_path_text: The chain works by linking three things across different carriers and months: identity, the existence of a completed trade credential, and the later legal-career context. First, the family-home scene during the November visit shows Jasmine physically handling an older framed certificate or binder from a South Miami vocational program while with her parents, making the credential part of her real life rather than a floating document. Second, the close document photo provides readable text with Jasmine Robinson's name, a South Miami institution reference, and a completion date or term on a certificate for a practical trade field, establishing that she actually completed a trade certificate. Third, a screenshot from a later cleanup or phone note shows a saved reminder or message about dropping off old school materials from that same program, reinforcing that the trade training is an earlier chapter and linking the South Miami credential to Jasmine by name again. Fourth, a separate work-context photo in downtown Miami shows Jasmine in formal legal attire at a legal mixer or office event with visible attorney and bar-association cues, demonstrating her later legal career during the album window. Putting these together, an agent can infer that Jasmine holds a trade certificate and that this credential belongs to an earlier period than the legal-professional materials that dominate her current life.

The room has warm afternoon window light, floral curtains, and a lived-in family-home feel rather than a staged archive setup. Jasmine is dressed neatly but casually in a blouse and slacks, smiling as if explaining something to her parents. The certificate is not centered like a scan; it is partially obscured by old papers and a manila folder, yet its title area and institution line are readable. Family objects like serving dishes, a holiday tablecloth edge, and a pastry box make the scene plausible as a real visit. The camera captures both Jasmine's face and the certificate in the same frame, grounding the document to her identity and family context.

facts.text: She is a regular participant in group cycling rides around Miami, often joining weekend route meetups.
facts.reasoning_path_text: An agent can infer this fact by combining repeated activity scenes, recurring companions, route-specific location anchors, readable meetup text, and weekend-morning timing across separated months. First, the October causeway ride photo shows Jasmine herself in full cycling gear among multiple riders on the Rickenbacker Causeway with Tanya, establishing that she personally joins a real group ride rather than posing with a bike once. Second, a phone screenshot from the same month shows a route meetup thread naming the Rickenbacker ride, an early Saturday meet time, and Jasmine in the participant conversation, grounding the activity as an organized weekend meetup rather than an accidental gathering. Third, the January ride scene again shows Jasmine with bikes and riders, this time with Tanya and Renee in cooler-weather gear at the same route area, proving recurrence across months and with a consistent cycling social circle. Fourth, the post-ride brunch screenshot in January links the morning ride to a social after-ride meetup with the same rider friends, reinforcing that these are habitual group outings. Fifth, the June sunrise cycling gear photo at the causeway shows her prepared for another very early ride in summer conditions, with multiple helmets and road bikes clustered for departure, adding seasonal continuity and the characteristic early-morning routine. Sixth, the October 2012 meetup board scene returns to the same route context with a visible route note and assembled cyclists, closing a year-long pattern. Together these photos support both atomic claims: she participates in group cycling rides, and the activity recurs over many months in Miami, commonly on weekend route meetups.

key_photo_roles.planned_description: A wide outdoor action-group photo taken just after sunrise on the shoulder path of the Rickenbacker Causeway. Jasmine is clearly visible near the center, standing beside a road bike and still wearing a fitted cycling jersey, black padded shorts, fingerless gloves, and a vented helmet pushed up after the effort. Around her are six to eight other riders in mixed bright jerseys, clustered in the loose semicircle people naturally form after regrouping. Tanya Ellis is beside her with a white helmet and teal jersey, and the riders’ bikes overlap in the foreground with dropped handlebars, clipped shoes, and water bottles catching the low warm light. The bay water and guardrail run along one side, while the long causeway stretch and Miami skyline sit in the distance. The camera is held at chest height by another rider, giving a casual social-document feel rather than a posed portrait. Sweat-darkened fabric, sunglasses on helmets, and a few riders checking bike computers make it unmistakably a real group ride stop rather than a single-person workout.

The thread title at the top reads something like 'Sat Causeway Ride,' and several short messages stack below in the familiar rounded bubbles of an early-2010s smartphone messaging app. One message mentions meeting at the Rickenbacker toll plaza at 7:00 AM Saturday, another says coffee after the loop, and a reply from Jasmine confirms she is in. The screenshot includes the status bar and timestamp, reinforcing it as a captured planning artifact rather than fabricated overlay text. facts.text: She signs up for organized charity rides and community cycling events during the year.
facts.reasoning_path_text: First, a registration confirmation screenshot tied to Jasmine by name shows she actively registered for a named ride event rather than merely joining an informal group outing. Second, a real-world event photo from that same period places Jasmine physically at an organized cycling ride, with bib, branded backdrop, and other participants making it clear this is a formal event. Taken together, the chain supports both atomic claims: she participates in organized cycling events, and at least one of those rides is clearly charity- or community-oriented.

facts.text: Home baking is one of her recurring hobbies, and she regularly makes decorated cakes, cookies, and pastry batches to share with others.
facts.reasoning_path_text: Across four different months, the photos repeatedly place Jasmine in the same Brickell home kitchen with baking-specific tools, ingredients, and finished goods, showing this is not a one-off event but a sustained home activity. One image shows her actively icing decorated cookies in October; another in December shows multiple packed holiday cookie tins staged for delivery, tying the baking to sharing beyond her household. A third image in March shows her presenting a frosted birthday cake at a friend gathering, connecting her directly to decorated cakes made for others. A fourth image in August shows a pastry and cookie batch cooling and being boxed on an ordinary evening at home, indicating she continues baking even outside major holidays. The repeated location anchor, visible preparation/decorating steps, variety of baked items, and several transport or group-sharing contexts together support the inference that home baking is a recurring hobby and that she regularly makes decorated cakes, cookies, and pastries to share with other people.

key_photo_roles.evidence_role: Shows Jasmine personally baking and decorating cookies at home, establishing the hobby as her own hands-on activity rather than something purchased.
key_photo_roles.planned_description: In Jasmine's Brickell apartment kitchen, the camera looks diagonally across a bright granite counter toward her standing at the island in a casual top with her sleeves pushed up, concentrating as she pipes white icing onto a tray of leaf- and pumpkin-shaped sugar cookies. Afternoon window light comes from the side, catching the shine of metal cooling racks and the glossy bowls of orange, cream, and brown frosting. Beside her are a rolling pin dusted with flour, a half-used bag of powdered sugar, measuring spoons, a stand mixer in the background, and two completed rows of neatly decorated cookies already drying on parchment paper. A cardboard bakery box lies open at the far edge of the counter with tissue paper ready inside, suggesting the cookies are being packed up rather than just eaten at home. The framing is close enough to show Jasmine's face and hands clearly, but wide enough to establish the whole home-kitchen environment and the purposeful decorating process.
must_show: Jasmine visibly icing shaped sugar cookies
multiple already decorated fall-themed cookies on cooling racks
open bakery-style box or container waiting on the counter
home kitchen tools including mixer, rolling pin, and bowls of icing

key_photo_roles.evidence_role: Demonstrates repeated baking in larger batches and, crucially, that the output is being packed and labeled for sharing with neighbors and coworkers.
key_photo_roles.planned_description: A close home scene in the same Brickell kitchen shows a dining table and adjacent counter covered with holiday baking output rather than active cooking. Several round cookie tins sit open and partially filled with frosted cutout cookies, iced shortbread, and small bar pieces separated by wax paper. A handwritten note card leans against one tin, and a stack of ribbon, tape, and cellophane bags suggests Jasmine is preparing gifts or drop-offs. In the background, the familiar stand mixer and cooling racks from earlier scenes are still visible, but the emphasis is on quantity and packaging. Warm evening lamp light mixes with dim window reflections, making the setting feel like a real after-work preparation session. One tin is labeled for a neighbor and another for coworkers, while a third is still being packed. The camera angle is slightly above table height, making the repeated batches and organized distribution setup obvious, and the image clearly reads as homemade sweets being prepared to leave the apartment for other people.
must_show: several tins or boxes filled with assorted homemade cookies
packaging materials like ribbon, cellophane bags, or wax paper
handwritten labels or cards attached to at least one tin
same home kitchen background with cooling racks or mixer

key_photo_roles.evidence_role: Extends the pattern beyond cookies by showing Jasmine bringing a decorated cake she made for a friend-centered celebration.
key_photo_roles.planned_description: At a friend birthday dinner, the camera captures Jasmine stepping forward from the near side of a dining table while carrying a homemade round cake on a white pedestal stand. The cake is clearly decorated, with smooth frosted sides, piped borders, and a cluster of fresh berries and candles arranged on top. Jasmine is smiling toward the seated group, and the scene includes Carla Bennett near the table reacting to the cake, plus other dinner guests turning toward it. Restaurant-style plates, glasses, and a half-cleared meal indicate the dessert is arriving as part of a social celebration, not a bakery display case. Indoor evening lighting is warm and slightly dim, with the cake brightly catching the candlelight. The framing keeps Jasmine's face recognizable while also showing that she is the one physically bringing in the cake. The image makes the social use of her baking obvious: this is a polished homemade dessert contributed to a shared gathering, consistent with someone who regularly bakes for others.

birthday dinner table with guests reacting to the cake

key_photo_roles.evidence_role: Confirms the hobby continues months later in ordinary home life and includes pastry batches prepared in shareable quantities.
key_photo_roles.planned_description: Back in the Brickell apartment on a humid late-summer evening, the kitchen counter is crowded with a smaller but clearly intentional baking project: a tray of golden hand pies or turnovers cooling beside rows of iced cookies, while Jasmine folds bakery boxes and slips parchment between layers. She is turned slightly toward the camera, one hand holding metal tongs and the other resting near an open pastry box, so it is clear she is managing the batch herself. A flour canister, butter wrappers, silicone pastry brush, and a mixing bowl with streaks of glaze show recent preparation rather than staged serving. The lighting comes from overhead kitchen fixtures, giving the scene a practical weekday feel rather than a party atmosphere. Through the framing, the viewer can see multiple finished items, packing materials, and enough quantity to exceed a single-person snack. The same home kitchen features reappear, reinforcing continuity across months while also adding variety through pastry-focused output instead of only decorated cookies or cakes.

rows of iced cookies or other finished sweets beside them

facts.text: She keeps an active social calendar centered on dinners, birthdays, brunches, and group nights out with a consistent circle of friends.
facts.reasoning_path_text: First, identify Jasmine as a recurring participant in several social gatherings across the album window. Next, compare the faces appearing with her in different months and note that Monique Harris, Natalie Price, Andre Holloway, and Carla Bennett recur across multiple separate outings rather than only one isolated event. Then use the scene context in each image—restaurant table settings, birthday cake and candles, daylight brunch plates, and evening drinks/bar lighting—to classify the gatherings as dinners, birthdays, brunches, and nights out. Repeated use of the same social districts, especially Coconut Grove and Miami Beach Marina, strengthens the interpretation that these are habitual friend meetups rather than random one-off encounters. The screenshot of the reservation thread adds direct planning evidence that the same named friends are coordinating another dinner. Taken together, the cross-month recurrence of the same non-family faces in varied but clearly social settings supports the conclusion that Jasmine maintains an active social calendar built around a consistent friend circle.

facts.text: She often appears at professional mixers, bar events, and civic gatherings with other legal and business professionals.
facts.reasoning_path_text: The chain works by combining repeated real-world event scenes with readable event text and a small amount of schedule evidence across multiple months. First, several photos place Jasmine herself at evening receptions in business attire, standing with similarly dressed adults wearing name badges, holding drinks, and socializing in hotel-bar or downtown reception settings rather than private friend dinners. Second, readable signage and badge text tie those scenes to legal, bar-association, civic, and business-networking contexts, establishing that these are professional gatherings rather than generic nights out. Third, a screenshot and a paper program add explicit event naming and RSVP structure, showing these appearances are intentional entries in her calendar rather than one accidental overlap. Fourth, the months are spread from late 2011 through fall 2012, showing recurrence over time. Finally, the repeated presence of legal colleagues and a real-estate professional companion in those scenes supports the narrower inference that the surrounding network is composed of legal and business professionals. Taken together, the evidence supports both that she attends professional mixers or similar receptions and that the relevant crowd is legal and business oriented.

The event title is clearly networking-oriented and legal in tone, and the location line references a Brickell or downtown venue. Small neighboring entries such as court prep or client call remain partially visible but not fully readable, creating a believable busy attorney schedule. The visual design should look like the native calendar app, with pale gray hour divisions and one colored event block in the early evening. This image functions as a planning artifact: it does not prove attendance alone, but it shows Jasmine intentionally carved out weekday evening time for a professional legal mixer in the same urban district where later scene photos place her in cocktail attire. The screenshot should feel mundane, practical, and consistent with a working lawyer’s phone.

facts.text: She is comfortable hosting or contributing baked goods for group celebrations and office social occasions.
facts.reasoning_path_text: The evidence chain works by separating baking at home from actual social contribution, then reconnecting them across repeated contexts. First, one home-prep photo shows Jasmine personally packaging decorated treats in her Brickell kitchen for transport, with office-style containers and a sticky note indicating they are for coworkers rather than just for home consumption. Second, a workplace celebration scene in Downtown Miami shows Jasmine beside the same style of baked goods on a shared office table during a colleague birthday gathering, establishing that the homemade items are being brought into a group social setting at work. Third, a spring family brunch scene in Miami Gardens shows Jasmine again arriving or serving a baked dessert on a larger shared celebration table with relatives gathered around, demonstrating the same contribution behavior in a non-office group celebration. Fourth, a later summer office scene repeats the pattern with a different baked item, confirming that this is not a one-off holiday exception but a comfortable recurring habit. Across multiple months, multiple locations, and multiple carrier types, the agent can infer that Jasmine is at ease bringing or serving baked goods for group occasions, especially office and celebration contexts.

facts.text: She maintains a polished, court-appropriate wardrobe that frequently includes tailored dresses, blazers, and formal heels.
facts.reasoning_path_text: The inference comes from repeated, temporally separated visual appearances of Jasmine in clearly formal professional clothing rather than one isolated dressed-up occasion. First, a weekday courthouse-commute style image in October shows her personally wearing a fitted sheath dress, structured blazer, closed-toe heels, and carrying a legal work bag and case files in Downtown Miami, tying the outfit to work rather than leisure. Second, a December professional mixer scene at the same downtown work anchor shows her again in a different tailored outfit with heels among attorneys and legal contacts, confirming that this is a recurring presentation style across formal professional events. Third, a June networking reception in Coral Gables shows the same polished silhouette in warm-weather variation—another tailored dress, blazer, and formal heels—demonstrating continuity even months later and in a separate business-social setting. Fourth, a September home-entryway preparation scene adds wardrobe-context evidence by showing multiple court-appropriate pieces actually in active rotation: pressed blazer, tailored dress, heels, legal tote, and neatly staged case materials ready for departure. Taken together across four months and two recurring locations, the chain supports the stable conclusion that Jasmine regularly maintains a polished, court-appropriate wardrobe rather than occasionally dressing up.

facts.text: She spends time in waterfront and outdoor social settings around Miami, especially for casual meetups and post-ride hangouts.
facts.reasoning_path_text: An agent can infer this fact by combining repeated lived scenes rather than relying on one explicit statement. First, the January brunch photo shows Jasmine with cycling friends in an outdoor bayside setting at Coconut Grove immediately after a ride, with helmets, bikes, coffee, and open-air seating indicating that social time continues after exercise. Second, the February marina café scene places Jasmine again near the water in a casual social setting, this time after a coastal ride, showing the same pattern in a different month and location. Third, the April waterfront hangout on the Rickenbacker Causeway reinforces that this is not a one-off event but a recurring routine tied to Miami’s outdoor cycling culture, with bikes parked nearby and the group lingering socially by the bay. Fourth, the June dinner scene at Miami Beach Marina extends the pattern beyond morning rides into warm-weather waterfront socializing with her partner, proving she also chooses waterside Miami settings for casual meetups and evenings out. Across four photos over six months, with repeated Miami waterfront anchors, recurring social companions, and cycling-adjacent cues, the evidence supports a stable routine of spending time in outdoor and waterfront social settings around Miami.

key_photo_roles.planned_description: A candid late-morning outdoor brunch photo shows Jasmine Robinson seated at a weathered café table on a shaded waterfront patio in Coconut Grove with Tanya Ellis and Renee Walters after a weekend ride. Jasmine is still in fitted cycling shorts and a zip-front jersey with her helmet resting on the spare chair beside her, while Tanya and Renee also have helmets and gloves nearby. Two road bikes are leaned against a low railing behind them, and beyond the railing the bay water and a few docked sailboats are visible in bright winter sunlight. The table holds iced coffees, a basket with toast, water glasses, and a folded route map or phone on the tabletop. The camera angle is eye level from across the table, catching Jasmine mid-laugh and the women in relaxed conversation rather than posing formally. In the background, a small sign or menu board includes the location wording for Coconut Grove, grounding the scene in Miami and making the image read as a routine post-ride social stop instead of a generic restaurant visit.

cycling helmets or gloves on the table or chairs
two or more road bikes visible near a railing
waterfront backdrop with bay water or docked boats

The group appears to have finished a ride and settled into an easy conversation over cold drinks and sandwiches. Several road bikes are parked together near the edge of the patio, and behind them are masts, slips, and the bright blue water of the marina in midday sun. The scene feels unhurried and social, with waterfront scenery clearly functioning as the setting for hanging out after an active morning.

evaluation_targets.reasoning_path_text: An agent can infer this fact by combining repeated activity scenes, recurring companions, route-specific location anchors, readable meetup text, and weekend-morning timing across separated months. First, the October causeway ride photo shows Jasmine herself in full cycling gear among multiple riders on the Rickenbacker Causeway with Tanya, establishing that she personally joins a real group ride rather than posing with a bike once. Second, a phone screenshot from the same month shows a route meetup thread naming the Rickenbacker ride, an early Saturday meet time, and Jasmine in the participant conversation, grounding the activity as an organized weekend meetup rather than an accidental gathering. Third, the January ride scene again shows Jasmine with bikes and riders, this time with Tanya and Renee in cooler-weather gear at the same route area, proving recurrence across months and with a consistent cycling social circle. Fourth, the post-ride brunch screenshot in January links the morning ride to a social after-ride meetup with the same rider friends, reinforcing that these are habitual group outings. Fifth, the June sunrise cycling gear photo at the causeway shows her prepared for another very early ride in summer conditions, with multiple helmets and road bikes clustered for departure, adding seasonal continuity and the characteristic early-morning routine. Sixth, the October 2012 meetup board scene returns to the same route context with a visible route note and assembled cyclists, closing a year-long pattern. Together these photos support both atomic claims: she participates in group cycling rides, and the activity recurs over many months in Miami, commonly on weekend route meetups.

key_photo_roles.evidence_role: Grounded visual anchor showing Jasmine physically attending and riding in a formal event, not just registering online.
key_photo_roles.planned_description: Jasmine stands near an event staging area outside the Miami Beach Convention Center in early daylight, wearing a fitted cycling jersey, helmet pushed back, fingerless gloves, and a numbered bib pinned across the front of her jersey. Her face is clearly visible and slightly flushed from exertion, and she is holding her bike by the handlebars while smiling with another rider nearby in the background. Behind her is a temporary printed backdrop and fencing typical of organized events, including sponsor logos, a start or check-in banner, and clustered participants in helmets waiting with bikes. The pavement is bright from morning sun, and the angle is eye level, like a friend stepped back to capture her full upper body plus the event setup. Visible artifacts include wristbands, bib numbers on other riders, water tables, and branded feather flags. The scene must read unmistakably as a formal organized ride rather than a casual weekend meetup because the signage, crowd control barriers, and bib infrastructure are all visible at once.
must_show: Jasmine visibly wearing a numbered bike event bib

The text block names Jasmine Robinson as the registered rider and includes an event title that signals a community or civic cycling ride rather than a race. The screen also shows a March 2012 date, a start location field, and a button or label for rider details. A small icon row and status bar at top make it look like a normal iPhone-era screen capture. The event wording should feel local and community-centered, not identical to the November charity registration, so the two artifacts read as distinct sign-ups over time. The composition remains simple and legible: mostly white screen, black and blue interface elements, and a registration badge or order number near the lower half. This photo contributes recurrence through OCR and time separation, proving that the November event was not a one-off.

She is recognizable in sunglasses pushed up on her helmet and a bright cycling jersey, with a paper bib or plate number attached to the handlebars or jersey. Around her are many other cyclists in staggered clusters, and a temporary roadside sign or banner references the organized ride and community aspect of the event. The bay water and long causeway stretch in the background under crisp morning light, making the Miami route recognizable and consistent with her known riding context. The vantage is slightly off-center, showing her from knees up with bike, other riders, volunteer table, cones, and event signage all in one frame. evaluation_targets.parent_fact_text: Home baking is one of her recurring hobbies, and she regularly makes decorated cakes, cookies, and pastry batches to share with others.
evaluation_targets.reasoning_path_text: Across four different months, the photos repeatedly place Jasmine in the same Brickell home kitchen with baking-specific tools, ingredients, and finished goods, showing this is not a one-off event but a sustained home activity. One image shows her actively icing decorated cookies in October; another in December shows multiple packed holiday cookie tins staged for delivery, tying the baking to sharing beyond her household. A third image in March shows her presenting a frosted birthday cake at a friend gathering, connecting her directly to decorated cakes made for others. A fourth image in August shows a pastry and cookie batch cooling and being boxed on an ordinary evening at home, indicating she continues baking even outside major holidays. The repeated location anchor, visible preparation/decorating steps, variety of baked items, and several transport or group-sharing contexts together support the inference that home baking is a recurring hobby and that she regularly makes decorated cakes, cookies, and pastries to share with other people.

key_photo_roles.evidence_role: Shows Jasmine personally baking and decorating cookies at home, establishing the hobby as her own hands-on activity rather than something purchased.
key_photo_roles.planned_description: In Jasmine's Brickell apartment kitchen, the camera looks diagonally across a bright granite counter toward her standing at the island in a casual top with her sleeves pushed up, concentrating as she pipes white icing onto a tray of leaf- and pumpkin-shaped sugar cookies. Afternoon window light comes from the side, catching the shine of metal cooling racks and the glossy bowls of orange, cream, and brown frosting. Beside her are a rolling pin dusted with flour, a half-used bag of powdered sugar, measuring spoons, a stand mixer in the background, and two completed rows of neatly decorated cookies already drying on parchment paper. A cardboard bakery box lies open at the far edge of the counter with tissue paper ready inside, suggesting the cookies are being packed up rather than just eaten at home. The framing is close enough to show Jasmine's face and hands clearly, but wide enough to establish the whole home-kitchen environment and the purposeful decorating process.
must_show: Jasmine visibly icing shaped sugar cookies
multiple already decorated fall-themed cookies on cooling racks
open bakery-style box or container waiting on the counter
home kitchen tools including mixer, rolling pin, and bowls of icing

key_photo_roles.evidence_role: Demonstrates repeated baking in larger batches and, crucially, that the output is being packed and labeled for sharing with neighbors and coworkers.
key_photo_roles.planned_description: A close home scene in the same Brickell kitchen shows a dining table and adjacent counter covered with holiday baking output rather than active cooking. Several round cookie tins sit open and partially filled with frosted cutout cookies, iced shortbread, and small bar pieces separated by wax paper. A handwritten note card leans against one tin, and a stack of ribbon, tape, and cellophane bags suggests Jasmine is preparing gifts or drop-offs. In the background, the familiar stand mixer and cooling racks from earlier scenes are still visible, but the emphasis is on quantity and packaging. Warm evening lamp light mixes with dim window reflections, making the setting feel like a real after-work preparation session. One tin is labeled for a neighbor and another for coworkers, while a third is still being packed. The camera angle is slightly above table height, making the repeated batches and organized distribution setup obvious, and the image clearly reads as homemade sweets being prepared to leave the apartment for other people.
must_show: several tins or boxes filled with assorted homemade cookies
packaging materials like ribbon, cellophane bags, or wax paper

The cake is clearly decorated, with smooth frosted sides, piped borders, and a cluster of fresh berries and candles arranged on top. Jasmine is smiling toward the seated group, and the scene includes Carla Bennett near the table reacting to the cake, plus other dinner guests turning toward it. Restaurant-style plates, glasses, and a half-cleared meal indicate the dessert is arriving as part of a social celebration, not a bakery display case. Indoor evening lighting is warm and slightly dim, with the cake brightly catching the candlelight. The framing keeps Jasmine's face recognizable while also showing that she is the one physically bringing in the cake. The image makes the social use of her baking obvious: this is a polished homemade dessert contributed to a shared gathering, consistent with someone who regularly bakes for others.

She is turned slightly toward the camera, one hand holding metal tongs and the other resting near an open pastry box, so it is clear she is managing the batch herself. A flour canister, butter wrappers, silicone pastry brush, and a mixing bowl with streaks of glaze show recent preparation rather than staged serving. The lighting comes from overhead kitchen fixtures, giving the scene a practical weekday feel rather than a party atmosphere. Through the framing, the viewer can see multiple finished items, packing materials, and enough quantity to exceed a single-person snack. The same home kitchen features reappear, reinforcing continuity across months while also adding variety through pastry-focused output instead of only decorated cookies or cakes.

evaluation_targets.gt_text: She frequently socializes with a consistent friend group.
evaluation_targets.parent_fact_text: She keeps an active social calendar centered on dinners, birthdays, brunches, and group nights out with a consistent circle of friends.
evaluation_targets.reasoning_path_text: First, identify Jasmine as a recurring participant in several social gatherings across the album window. Next, compare the faces appearing with her in different months and note that Monique Harris, Natalie Price, Andre Holloway, and Carla Bennett recur across multiple separate outings rather than only one isolated event. Then use the scene context in each image—restaurant table settings, birthday cake and candles, daylight brunch plates, and evening drinks/bar lighting—to classify the gatherings as dinners, birthdays, brunches, and nights out. Repeated use of the same social districts, especially Coconut Grove and Miami Beach Marina, strengthens the interpretation that these are habitual friend meetups rather than random one-off encounters. The screenshot of the reservation thread adds direct planning evidence that the same named friends are coordinating another dinner. Taken together, the cross-month recurrence of the same non-family faces in varied but clearly social settings supports the conclusion that Jasmine maintains an active social calendar built around a consistent friend circle.

Jasmine is visible in a polished sleeveless dress, smiling toward the camera while a frosted birthday cake with several lit candles sits near the center of the table. Monique Harris is on one side of her and Andre Holloway is across from them, both clearly recognizable and engaged in the celebration. The tabletop is crowded with water glasses, wine stems, appetizer plates, folded napkins, a phone set down beside a clutch purse, and a small dessert plate with a cake knife. In the dim background there are other diners, warm hanging lights, and a partial restaurant wall sign that places the scene in Coconut Grove. The image should feel like a candid but composed friend-group celebration, with everyone turned inward toward the same event and the visual focus anchored by the cake and the repeated friend faces.

Jasmine sits at the near side of a round table in sunglasses pushed up on her head, leaning in toward Natalie Price and Carla Bennett, who are seated beside her and smiling as if someone has just suggested a group picture before eating. evaluation_targets.gt_text: She attends professional networking or mixer events.
evaluation_targets.parent_fact_text: She often appears at professional mixers, bar events, and civic gatherings with other legal and business professionals.
evaluation_targets.reasoning_path_text: The chain works by combining repeated real-world event scenes with readable event text and a small amount of schedule evidence across multiple months. First, several photos place Jasmine herself at evening receptions in business attire, standing with similarly dressed adults wearing name badges, holding drinks, and socializing in hotel-bar or downtown reception settings rather than private friend dinners. Second, readable signage and badge text tie those scenes to legal, bar-association, civic, and business-networking contexts, establishing that these are professional gatherings rather than generic nights out. Third, a screenshot and a paper program add explicit event naming and RSVP structure, showing these appearances are intentional entries in her calendar rather than one accidental overlap. Fourth, the months are spread from late 2011 through fall 2012, showing recurrence over time. Finally, the repeated presence of legal colleagues and a real-estate professional companion in those scenes supports the narrower inference that the surrounding network is composed of legal and business professionals. Taken together, the evidence supports both that she attends professional mixers or similar receptions and that the relevant crowd is legal and business oriented.

key_photo_roles.evidence_role: Shows Jasmine intentionally schedules a legal networking happy hour as part of her normal weekday routine.
key_photo_roles.planned_description: A smartphone screenshot of Jasmine’s calendar in month view for a weekday in late October, captured straight on with the iPhone status bar visible. The highlighted evening block shows a scheduled after-work event in downtown Miami, surrounded by ordinary workday entries that make it feel like a real personal calendar rather than a staged note. The event title is clearly networking-oriented and legal in tone, and the location line references a Brickell or downtown venue. Small neighboring entries such as court prep or client call remain partially visible but not fully readable, creating a believable busy attorney schedule. The visual design should look like the native calendar app, with pale gray hour divisions and one colored event block in the early evening. This image functions as a planning artifact: it does not prove attendance alone, but it shows Jasmine intentionally carved out weekday evening time for a professional legal mixer in the same urban district where later scene photos place her in cocktail attire. The screenshot should feel mundane, practical, and consistent with a working lawyer’s phone.

evaluation_targets.gt_text: She brings or serves baked goods at group occasions.
evaluation_targets.parent_fact_text: She is comfortable hosting or contributing baked goods for group celebrations and office social occasions.
evaluation_targets.reasoning_path_text: The evidence chain works by separating baking at home from actual social contribution, then reconnecting them across repeated contexts. First, one home-prep photo shows Jasmine personally packaging decorated treats in her Brickell kitchen for transport, with office-style containers and a sticky note indicating they are for coworkers rather than just for home consumption. Second, a workplace celebration scene in Downtown Miami shows Jasmine beside the same style of baked goods on a shared office table during a colleague birthday gathering, establishing that the homemade items are being brought into a group social setting at work. Third, a spring family brunch scene in Miami Gardens shows Jasmine again arriving or serving a baked dessert on a larger shared celebration table with relatives gathered around, demonstrating the same contribution behavior in a non-office group celebration. Fourth, a later summer office scene repeats the pattern with a different baked item, confirming that this is not a one-off holiday exception but a comfortable recurring habit. Across multiple months, multiple locations, and multiple carrier types, the agent can infer that Jasmine is at ease bringing or serving baked goods for group occasions, especially office and celebration contexts.

key_photo_roles.evidence_role: Shows Jasmine personally preparing and packaging homemade baked goods for transport to a shared social destination rather than just baking for herself at home.
key_photo_roles.planned_description: In Jasmine’s Brickell apartment kitchen, the camera looks slightly down from the doorway toward a long counter where she is actively packing baked goods to take out of the house. Jasmine is clearly recognizable, wearing casual home clothes with an apron tied at the waist, leaning over open bakery-style boxes and a plastic cupcake carrier. On cooling racks beside her are iced sugar cookies and a neat row of frosted cupcakes in coordinated colors, with a piping bag, offset spatula, parchment, and measuring cups still visible so the scene reads as fresh prep rather than store-bought pickup. Morning light from a window hits the counter and stainless appliances, while a handbag and car keys sit nearby as if she is about to leave. One lidded container already has a handwritten sticky note attached for the office break room. The composition should show the kitchen as lived-in but tidy, with enough detail to connect homemade baking, packaging, and intended sharing outside the home.
must_show: Jasmine packing frosted cupcakes and decorated cookies into transport boxes
cooling rack and baking tools still on the kitchen counter
handwritten sticky note on a box for the office break room
handbag or keys placed beside the packed dessert carrier

A long shared table is covered with paper plates, plastic forks, napkins, a couple of wrapped gifts, and a homemade dessert spread that includes decorated cookies and frosted cupcakes matching the style seen in the home kitchen photo. Jasmine stands beside the table in polished office attire, smiling and slightly turned toward coworkers as if she has just set the boxes down or is helping serve. At least Felicia Grant and Marcus Levin are visible in business clothes among a small group, making this read clearly as an office social occasion rather than a private dinner. There should be fluorescent indoor lighting, glass partitions or file cabinets in the background, and a small birthday card or tabletop sign near the desserts. The image must feel communal, with the treats integrated into a shared celebration setup rather than sitting untouched off to the side.

The interface should look like a realistic 2012 mobile calendar app, not overly modern, with one entry reading like a family or brunch plan and a second note below it specifying dessert transport or pickup from home. The wording should be short and natural, something a busy professional would actually type for herself. The screenshot should include the day grid or agenda list, time blocks, and the date in April, with no extra clutter. The visible text should suggest a shared gathering and Jasmine’s role in supplying a baked item, without mechanically restating the benchmark fact. A small location line or note field can reference Miami Gardens or brunch, and the phone status bar should make clear it is a contemporaneous personal planning artifact. This adds explicit text evidence that the baked dessert was intended for a social group event before the corresponding family scene occurs.
must_show: mobile calendar interface with an April Sunday agenda
calendar entry for family brunch
separate note indicating Jasmine is bringing cake or dessert
date and time blocks visible on the screenshot

key_photo_roles.evidence_role: Shows Jasmine contributing and serving baked dessert in a group family celebration context, extending the pattern beyond the workplace.
key_photo_roles.planned_description: The camera captures a bright spring family brunch in Miami Gardens from the side of the dining table, showing a fully social context rather than an isolated food close-up. Jasmine is recognizable and positioned near the center of the table, either setting down or slicing a homemade cake on a pedestal stand while her parents and at least one other relative or partner sit or stand nearby. The table is laid for a group meal with serving dishes, juice glasses, flowers, and brunch foods, and the cake looks homemade and seasonally polished, not bakery boxed. Natural daylight enters from a nearby window, giving the room a warm late-morning feel. A card, flowers, or Easter-adjacent spring décor can help signal that this is a celebratory gathering rather than an ordinary lunch. The framing should show enough of the room to read as a family home and enough of Jasmine’s interaction with the dessert to show she is contributing and comfortable serving it into the shared event. Faces should be engaged with the table, making the baked item part of the social occasion itself.
must_show: Jasmine placing or slicing a homemade cake at a family brunch table
multiple family members gathered around the same table
shared meal setting with brunch dishes and spring decor
cake presented on a serving stand rather than in a bakery box

key_photo_roles.evidence_role: Repeats the office celebration pattern in a later month, showing that Jasmine regularly brings baked goods into shared workplace social occasions.
key_photo_roles.planned_description: Inside the same Downtown Miami office environment several months later, the camera focuses on a summer birthday gathering around a conference table, this time centered on a tray of cupcakes that Jasmine has evidently brought in. The room has office neutrals, a glass wall, case files or legal pads pushed aside, and a cluster of coworkers pausing from the workday. Jasmine is visible near the cupcakes with one hand on the tray or passing out napkins, looking relaxed and socially engaged rather than formal or detached. A small birthday balloon, store-bought candles, and disposable plates mark the event as an office social occasion, while the cupcakes themselves have a homemade look with slightly varied frosting swirls and a reusable carrier nearby. Felicia Grant or another colleague can be seen reaching toward the dessert table, reinforcing shared participation. Afternoon indoor light and business attire should make the weekday work setting obvious. Because this scene repeats the work-dessert contribution pattern in a different month and with a different baked item, it supports the conclusion that Jasmine does this comfortably and more than once.
must_show: homemade-style cupcake tray on a conference or break-room table
Jasmine helping serve or standing immediately beside the tray
office coworkers in business attire gathered around
birthday cue such as candles, balloon, or small party plates

ambient_support_needs.description: Additional casual kitchen photos at loc_001 with cooling racks, mixing bowls, and decorated batches would reinforce that Brickell is her regular baking base.
Ambient office happy hour or break-room photos at loc_002 would make shared workplace celebrations feel like a normal recurring part of her routine.
Repeated appearance of the same cupcake carrier, pastry box, or ribboned cookie tin across months would strengthen cross-photo linkage between preparation and contribution scenes.

evaluation_targets.gt_text: She regularly wears formal professional clothing.
evaluation_targets.parent_fact_text: She maintains a polished, court-appropriate wardrobe that frequently includes tailored dresses, blazers, and formal heels.
evaluation_targets.reasoning_path_text: The inference comes from repeated, temporally separated visual appearances of Jasmine in clearly formal professional clothing rather than one isolated dressed-up occasion. First, a weekday courthouse-commute style image in October shows her personally wearing a fitted sheath dress, structured blazer, closed-toe heels, and carrying a legal work bag and case files in Downtown Miami, tying the outfit to work rather than leisure. Second, a December professional mixer scene at the same downtown work anchor shows her again in a different tailored outfit with heels among attorneys and legal contacts, confirming that this is a recurring presentation style across formal professional events. Third, a June networking reception in Coral Gables shows the same polished silhouette in warm-weather variation—another tailored dress, blazer, and formal heels—demonstrating continuity even months later and in a separate business-social setting. Fourth, a September home-entryway preparation scene adds wardrobe-context evidence by showing multiple court-appropriate pieces actually in active rotation: pressed blazer, tailored dress, heels, legal tote, and neatly staged case materials ready for departure. Taken together across four months and two recurring locations, the chain supports the stable conclusion that Jasmine regularly maintains a polished, court-appropriate wardrobe rather than occasionally dressing up.

key_photo_roles.evidence_role: Establishes Jasmine personally wearing a court-appropriate professional outfit in a real weekday work context.
key_photo_roles.planned_description: A street-level weekday image catches Jasmine just outside a Downtown Miami office or courthouse corridor entrance, walking toward the camera at a slight angle with a composed, purposeful expression. She is clearly recognizable and dressed in a knee-length tailored sheath dress in a solid dark tone, topped with a fitted blazer that sits sharply at the shoulders and waist. On her feet are polished closed-toe heels appropriate for court rather than party wear. One hand holds a structured leather work tote; the other grips a slim stack of legal folders and a notepad. The light is bright late-morning Miami sun, but the building overhang throws partial shade across the sidewalk, making the outfit details visible. Behind her are reflective glass doors, a stone facade, and a civic-looking urban block with other professionals passing in business clothing. The camera viewpoint is chest-height from across the sidewalk, wide enough to show her full outfit from blazer to heels, plus the work materials that anchor the scene in a legal weekday context.
must_show: tailored knee-length dress with structured blazer on Jasmine
closed-toe formal heels clearly visible
structured leather work tote and legal file folders
downtown office or courthouse entrance setting

key_photo_roles.evidence_role: Shows the same polished wardrobe pattern recurring at a separate professional event two months later.
key_photo_roles.planned_description: This evening reception photo is taken indoors at a downtown Miami legal holiday mixer, framed from several feet away so Jasmine is visible head to toe while standing with two other professionals near a high cocktail table. She is again the central visual anchor, wearing a different but equally formal outfit: a tailored dress with a clean silhouette, a cropped blazer or sharply cut jacket layered over it, and elegant mid-height heels that read as professional rather than festive nightclub shoes. She holds a drink in one hand and a small clutch or phone in the other, while a name badge or event lanyard sits discreetly at her neckline. The room has warm amber event lighting, glassware reflections, and holiday decor limited to tasteful white lights and greenery. In the background, other guests are also dressed in suits and business dresses, reinforcing the legal networking context. The camera angle is slightly below eye level from across the room, showing that Jasmine’s outfit is polished, intentional, and fully assembled for a professional social setting rather than casual office wear.
must_show: Jasmine in a second tailored dress-and-blazer combination
formal heels visible below the hemline
professional mixer environment with other business-dressed attendees
cocktail table or event badge indicating networking context

key_photo_roles.evidence_role: Confirms that even in a later month and different venue, Jasmine still defaults to polished formal professional clothing.
key_photo_roles.planned_description: A summer networking reception scene in Coral Gables shows Jasmine standing near the edge of a bar-area conversation cluster, turned three-quarters toward the camera so both her face and full outfit are visible. Despite the June heat implied by the season, her clothing remains distinctly professional: a sleeveless but tailored dress in a refined neutral color, topped with a lightweight fitted blazer draped naturally on her shoulders or worn properly, and paired with sleek closed-toe or conservative peep-toe formal heels suitable for business networking. She carries a structured handbag and wears understated jewelry, with her grooming neat and courtroom-professional. The setting includes polished wood, low indoor lighting, and a semi-upscale reception backdrop with professionals mingling over drinks after work. Nearby attendees wear business casual to formal attire, but Jasmine’s look is notably more precise and legal-professional. The camera captures her from standing height across the room, with enough lower-frame space to make the shoes and hemline unmistakable. The image reads as a real after-hours legal networking event, not a fashion portrait, because of the surrounding glasses, name tags, and conversation posture.
must_show: lightweight tailored dress with blazer in a summer business setting
formal heels visible as part of the outfit
structured handbag and neat professional grooming
networking reception crowd in business attire

key_photo_roles.evidence_role: Adds home-routine context showing that tailored professional pieces and formal heels are part of her regular getting-ready setup, not borrowed event wear.
key_photo_roles.planned_description: A home entryway mirror-prep photograph taken in Brickell captures Jasmine moments before leaving for work or court, using the mirror to check her appearance. The camera sees both her reflection and the surrounding staging area, making the wardrobe evidence feel lived-in rather than posed. She is already dressed in a crisp fitted blazer over a tailored dress, with one foot in a formal heel and the second heel either being fastened or placed neatly beside a narrow entry bench. On the bench or console table sits a structured black work tote, a manila case folder, a legal pad, a compact umbrella, and a set of keys. A garment bag or spare blazer hangs from a hook nearby, and the floor area is tidy enough that the heels read as regularly used office shoes instead of special-event footwear. Morning window light spills in from the side, creating bright natural illumination on the jacket seams and shoe shape. The viewpoint is slightly off-center, as if another person or a timer captured an authentic pre-departure moment. This image grounds the pattern by showing multiple court-appropriate wardrobe components in active daily rotation at home.
must_show: Jasmine reflected in mirror wearing blazer and tailored dress
formal heels at her feet or being put on
structured work tote with legal folder or legal pad nearby
entryway bench or console with additional polished workwear items

ambient_support_needs.description: Additional ambient office and bar-event photos at loc_002 would reinforce that Downtown Miami is a recurring professional setting where the same polished clothing pattern appears.
Other album images showing Jasmine in weekday professional contexts with her hair, makeup, and accessories consistently coordinated would strengthen the stable presentation signal.
Background photos from legal mixers and civic gatherings with colleagues in business attire would make her formal wardrobe read as normal routine context rather than a one-off special occasion.

evaluation_targets.gt_text: She regularly socializes in outdoor or waterfront Miami settings.
evaluation_targets.parent_fact_text: She spends time in waterfront and outdoor social settings around Miami, especially for casual meetups and post-ride hangouts.
evaluation_targets.reasoning_path_text: An agent can infer this fact by combining repeated lived scenes rather than relying on one explicit statement. First, the January brunch photo shows Jasmine with cycling friends in an outdoor bayside setting at Coconut Grove immediately after a ride, with helmets, bikes, coffee, and open-air seating indicating that social time continues after exercise. Second, the February marina café scene places Jasmine again near the water in a casual social setting, this time after a coastal ride, showing the same pattern in a different month and location. Third, the April waterfront hangout on the Rickenbacker Causeway reinforces that this is not a one-off event but a recurring routine tied to Miami’s outdoor cycling culture, with bikes parked nearby and the group lingering socially by the bay. Fourth, the June dinner scene at Miami Beach Marina extends the pattern beyond morning rides into warm-weather waterfront socializing with her partner, proving she also chooses waterside Miami settings for casual meetups and evenings out. Across four photos over six months, with repeated Miami waterfront anchors, recurring social companions, and cycling-adjacent cues, the evidence supports a stable routine of spending time in outdoor and waterfront social settings around Miami.

key_photo_roles.evidence_role: Shows Jasmine using a Miami waterfront outdoor café as a casual social hangout immediately after a group ride.
key_photo_roles.planned_description: A candid late-morning outdoor brunch photo shows Jasmine Robinson seated at a weathered café table on a shaded waterfront patio in Coconut Grove with Tanya Ellis and Renee Walters after a weekend ride. Jasmine is still in fitted cycling shorts and a zip-front jersey with her helmet resting on the spare chair beside her, while Tanya and Renee also have helmets and gloves nearby. Two road bikes are leaned against a low railing behind them, and beyond the railing the bay water and a few docked sailboats are visible in bright winter sunlight. The table holds iced coffees, a basket with toast, water glasses, and a folded route map or phone on the tabletop. The camera angle is eye level from across the table, catching Jasmine mid-laugh and the women in relaxed conversation rather than posing formally. In the background, a small sign or menu board includes the location wording for Coconut Grove, grounding the scene in Miami and making the image read as a routine post-ride social stop instead of a generic restaurant visit.
must_show: owner seated outdoors with Tanya and Renee at a brunch table
cycling helmets or gloves on the table or chairs
two or more road bikes visible near a railing
waterfront backdrop with bay water or docked boats

key_photo_roles.evidence_role: Adds a second month and second waterfront Miami location where Jasmine socializes casually in a post-ride setting.
key_photo_roles.planned_description: This photo is taken from a few feet back on a sunny patio at Miami Beach Marina, showing Jasmine standing with a relaxed half-turn toward the camera while Darius Campbell and Tanya Ellis lean on a marina-side railing beside a casual lunch table. Jasmine is still dressed in a cycling jersey with sunglasses pushed up on her head, and a white bike helmet hangs from one hand while Darius is in a light casual button-down and Tanya has a hydration belt looped over her chair. The group appears to have finished a ride and settled into an easy conversation over cold drinks and sandwiches. Several road bikes are parked together near the edge of the patio, and behind them are masts, slips, and the bright blue water of the marina in midday sun. The framing should include the marina sign or branded awning with readable location text so the place is specific to Miami Beach rather than a generic dock. The scene feels unhurried and social, with waterfront scenery clearly functioning as the setting for hanging out after an active morning.
must_show: owner visible with Darius and Tanya at a marina-side casual table
parked road bikes and at least one bike helmet
marina water with docked boats or masts
readable Miami Beach Marina sign or awning

key_photo_roles.evidence_role: Reinforces a repeated Miami outdoor social routine connected to cycling, specifically lingering and socializing at a waterfront route location.
key_photo_roles.planned_description: A wide outdoor group photo captures Jasmine and a small cycling cluster paused along the Rickenbacker Causeway at a scenic bay overlook after a Saturday ride. Jasmine stands in the center with one foot down beside her road bike, helmet off and clipped to the handlebars, smiling with the loose posture of someone lingering rather than racing off. Tanya Ellis and Renee Walters are beside her with bikes angled into the frame, and a couple of takeaway coffee cups and a paper pastry bag rest on a low concrete barrier. The background opens onto broad water, a long sweep of causeway railing, and distant Miami skyline elements in clear spring light. The camera is slightly lower than eye level and far enough back to show the riders, bikes, and gathering place together. A roadside marker or sign with the wording Rickenbacker Causeway should be visible near the edge of the frame. The photo reads as a social pause built into the route: no finish line, no race staging, just riders chatting, snacking, and spending time outdoors by the water in a recognizably Miami setting.
must_show: owner with Tanya and Renee beside their bikes
coffee cups or pastry bag on a barrier or bench
expansive bay water and causeway overlook
readable Rickenbacker Causeway sign

key_photo_roles.evidence_role: Shows that Jasmine's waterfront socializing extends beyond rides into regular casual evening meetups in Miami.
key_photo_roles.planned_description: An early evening summer dinner photo shows Jasmine and Darius seated at a small outdoor table beside the docks at Miami Beach Marina as daylight fades to warm sunset tones. Jasmine is no longer in cycling gear; she wears a sleeveless polished dinner outfit suitable for a casual date night, while Darius sits opposite her with a drink in hand. The marina water reflects orange light behind them, and several docked boats and rope lines frame the background. Their table holds shared appetizers, two beverages, and a small candle or table lamp, making the scene intimate but relaxed rather than formal. The camera position is slightly off to the side, as if taken by a friend or server, and captures both people, the open-air seating, and enough dock context to make the waterfront setting unmistakable. A visible sign, menu board, or awning reading Miami Beach Marina anchors the location. This image broadens the pattern by showing Jasmine choosing the same kind of Miami waterfront environment for social time even when it is not directly tied to a ride, indicating a stable preference for outdoor waterside meetups.
must_show: owner and Darius seated at an outdoor marina dinner table
sunset or evening light over docked boats and water
plates and drinks suggesting a casual meal
readable Miami Beach Marina location text

ambient_support_needs.description: Additional casual album photos can repeatedly show the Rickenbacker Causeway or other bayside backdrops to strengthen the sense of a habitual Miami outdoor circuit.
Ambient images of friends gathered around café tables, bike racks, or marina railings would reinforce that these locations function as social meetup spaces rather than isolated scenic stops.
Recurring helmets, road bikes, water bottles, and post-ride coffee cups across months would help connect outdoor social time with her cycling routine.

Jasmine stands near him holding a drink, and behind them are holiday details like a small decorated tree, silver trays, cocktail napkins, and a buffet table with dessert tins that fit her holiday baking habits.
